claimed_contribution: The paper conceptualizes CRM at the customer-facing level as a systematic process spanning relationship initiation, maintenance, and termination.
It operationalizes and validates a formative, multidimensional measure of CRM process implementation, including nine subdimensions and higher-level stage indexes.
It empirically tests the CRM process-performance relationship and contingency effects of organizational alignment and CRM technology using cross-industry, cross-country data.
context: The study examines CRM in consumer markets, where firms manage many customers across multiple contact channels. Empirical data come from strategic business units in financial services, hospitality, online retailing, and power utilities in Austria, Germany, and Switzerland.
design_and_data: Study 1 used a cross-sectional survey (fall 2001) of executives responsible for CRM in 1,015 firms, yielding 211 usable SBU-level responses (21.1 percent). The authors built formative CRM indexes from 42 items and estimated models linking CRM initiation, maintenance, and termination to performance, including interactions with CRM-compatible organizational alignment and CRM technology. Perceptual performance was measured with four relative performance items, and objective performance used average ROA for 2001-2002, available for 98 firms (81 public, 17 nonpublic). A second cross-sectional sample from the same firm frame provided 95 additional responses for cross-validation.
main_findings: Greater CRM process implementation is positively associated with economic performance, with the strongest and most consistent effects at relationship maintenance and weaker support at initiation.
Effects for relationship termination are weak or inconsistent, including non-significant effects for perceptual performance.

CRM-compatible organizational alignment shows positive moderating effects mainly for initiation (and termination in perceptual models), whereas CRM technology moderation is mixed and often non-significant, with a negative effect for initiation in perceptual models.
one_line_summary: This paper develops and validates a customer-facing CRM process measure across initiation, maintenance, and termination stages and finds that higher implementation is moderately associated with better firm performance.
relation_to_prior_work: extends relationship marketing and customer asset perspectives by specifying CRM as a lifecycle process with initiation, maintenance, and termination stages at the individual customer relationship level. It supplements CAM and customer equity approaches by adding explicit management activities such as regain, up-sell/cross-sell, referral, and exit management.
research_gap: Prior work examined isolated CRM-related outcomes (for example, satisfaction, loyalty, and customer profitability heterogeneity) but provided little cross-firm evidence on what the CRM process itself consists of. The literature and practice also lacked a clear, validated measure of CRM implementation and evidence on when CRM improves performance.
research_question: What constitutes CRM process implementation at the customer-facing level, and how can it be measured in a theoretically grounded way across firms? The authors also ask whether greater implementation of CRM processes in initiation, maintenance, and termination is associated with stronger economic performance, and whether organizational alignment and CRM technology moderate that relationship.
